title: Chamomile bath for mild insomnia
subtitle: A relaxing herb bath to help an overtired body unwind
excerpt: A concentrated chamomile infusion added to a warm evening bath. Traditionally used for restlessness and difficulty unwinding in the evening. One of the gentlest and most accessible herbal sleep preparations.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), chamomile entry, nervine and antispasmodic properties, use for restlessness and sleeplessness. EMA HMPC monograph on Matricaria flos (2015), confirms topical and aromatic use for relaxation.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Prepare the concentrate while the bath is filling. Dried chamomile flowers keep 12 months in a sealed tin.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that simultaneously calms the nervous system and eases muscle tension, chamomile's characteristic combined action.

content.text: A strong chamomile infusion poured into an evening bath. Where the chamomile cup works from the inside, the bath works through the skin and through inhalation of the steam, the same

action that relaxes the gut after a meal relaxes the muscles and the nervous system when absorbed through a warm bath. Maud Grieve records chamomile's traditional use for restlessness and sleeplessness; the EMA's 2015 monograph confirms aromatic and topical use for relaxation. For anyone who finds taking herbs internally difficult, children old enough to bathe alone, people who dislike herbal teas, or nights when a cup of tea feels like too much effort, the chamomile bath is a reliable alternative.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Asteraceae allergy: chamomile is in the Asteraceae (daisy) family. Anyone with a known allergy to ragweed, chrysanthemum, calendula, or related plants should not use chamomile bath preparations without a patch test, add a small amount of the prepared infusion to the forearm and wait 24 hours before a full bath.

Bath temperature: keep the bath warm rather than hot (37, 38°C). Very hot baths are stimulating and counterproductive for sleep.

See your doctor for insomnia lasting more than four weeks or accompanied by mood changes.

items.prepNote: a large, generous amount, the bath dilutes the concentrate significantly
just off the boil, for the concentrated infusion that goes into the bath

content.text: Place {{dried-chamomile-flowers}} of dried chamomile flowers in the large saucepan. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over, put the lid on, and steep for 10 minutes. The liquid will be dark golden-yellow and smell strongly of chamomile's apple-honey character. Strain through a sieve directly into the bath as it fills with warm water. Soak for 15 to 20 minutes before going to bed.

One bath in the evening, 30 to 60 minutes before bed. Can be used nightly during a period of disrupted sleep or high stress. For children over two who have no Asteraceae allergy, a chamomile bath is a traditional sleep preparation, use half the quantity of flowers (25 g) for a child's bath.
When NOT to use this remedy

Skip chamomile bath preparations if you have a known Asteraceae allergy. See your doctor for insomnia lasting more than four weeks, insomnia accompanied by depression or anxiety, or if you suspect an underlying cause such as sleep apnoea.
